preamble: The voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ can undergo a process of lenition, specifically vocalization, where it weakens to a labio-velar approximant /w/. This approximant can then combine with a preceding vowel, often through diphthongization, to form a diphthong, such as /au/. This transformation is a common type of consonant weakening, where a fricative becomes less obstructed and eventually merges into a vowel sound.

examples.note: The Old English voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ in 'fugol' (pronounced /fuɣol/) vocalized to a labio-velar approximant /w/ in Middle English. This /w/ then combined with the preceding vowel to form a diphthong, which evolved into /aʊ/ in Modern English (as in /faʊl/), often broadly transcribed as /au/.

sources: Minkova, D. (1992). Phonology. In R. M. Hogg (Ed.), The Cambridge History of the English Language, Vol. 1: The Beginnings to 1066 (pp. 387-467). Cambridge University Press.
